content.text: AuditIfNotExists (AINE) is an auditing effect that checks whether a companion resource (or related resource) exists alongside the primary resource that triggered policy evaluation. If the companion is absent, AINE marks the primary resource as NonCompliant. Unlike Deny, AINE never blocks resource creation or updates — the resource is always allowed to proceed. Unlike DeployIfNotExists (DINE), AINE never deploys anything — it only reads and reports.

AINE is the correct tool when you want visibility into missing companion resources — VM extensions not installed, diagnostic settings not configured, soft delete not enabled — without forcing remediation. It pairs with a compliance dashboard or alerting pipeline that identifies gaps so platform engineers can investigate and remediate manually or through a separate DINE policy.

An AINE policy definition uses the AuditIfNotExists effect and requires a details block that specifies what companion resource to look for and what conditions it must satisfy. The type field names the companion resource type. The existenceCondition narrows the check — without it, any companion of that type counts as compliant even if it is misconfigured.
AINE requires no managed identity and no roleDefinitionIds — it never writes to Azure. The policy engine reads companion resource properties using its own read access, not a delegated identity.

The three effects share an auditing function but differ in what they check and whether they take action. Audit checks properties of the primary resource and marks it non-compliant if the IF condition matches. AINE checks for the existence of a companion resource. DINE also checks for the companion but deploys it if missing.
Enforce properties directly on the resource

The existenceCondition defines what makes a companion resource count as satisfying the policy. If omitted, any companion of the specified type causes the resource to be considered Compliant — even if it is pointing to the wrong workspace or in an incomplete state. Always include conditions that verify the companion is correctly configured for your governance requirement.
AuditIfNotExists covers a wide range of companion resource checks. These are the patterns most frequently seen in Azure landing zone and CIS benchmark implementations.
SQL server has Advanced Threat Protection
Full AINE Policy: Key Vault Diagnostic Settings

This example audits Key Vaults that do not have a diagnostic setting pointing to the required Log Analytics workspace. Resources created before the policy was assigned that lack diagnostic settings will surface as NonCompliant.
AINE assignments do not require a managed identity parameter or a location, because no deployment occurs. This makes assignment simpler than DINE — no role assignment step needed after creation.

# Create the policy definition
az policy definition create \
  --name audit-kv-diag-settings \
  --display-name 'Audit Key Vaults without diagnostic settings' \
  --rules @kv-aine-policy.json \
  --mode All \
  --management-group mg-corp

# Assign at management group scope — no --mi-system-assigned, no --location needed
az policy assignment create \
  --name audit-kv-diag \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --policy /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/audit-kv-diag-settings \
  --params '{
    "logAnalyticsWorkspaceId": { "value": "/subscriptions/11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111/resourceGroups/rg-monitoring/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/law-corp" }
  }'

# Trigger a compliance scan (results appear within ~15 minutes normally)
az policy state trigger-scan --management-group mg-corp

# View non-compliant Key Vaults
az policy state list \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'audit-kv-diag' and complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query '[].{KV:resourceId,Group:resourceGroup,Sub:subscriptionId}' \
  -o table

# View compliance summary across all resources
az policy state summarize \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'audit-kv-diag'" \
  --query 'results.resourceDetails'
Terraform: AINE Policy Definition and Assignment
Querying AINE Compliance with Resource Graph

// Non-compliant Key Vaults for the AINE assignment
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| where properties.policyDefinitionAction == 'auditifnotexists'
| where properties.policyAssignmentName == 'audit-kv-diag'
| project
    kvId        = tostring(properties.resourceId),
    sub         = subscriptionId,
    rg          = tostring(properties.resourceGroup),
    state       = tostring(properties.complianceState)
| order by sub asc, rg asc

// Count non-compliant Key Vaults by subscription
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policyAssignmentName == 'audit-kv-diag'
| summarize
    Compliant    = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Compliant'),
    NonCompliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant')
    by subscriptionId
| extend CompliancePct = round(100.0 * Compliant / (Compliant + NonCompliant), 1)
| order by CompliancePct asc

// Which policy assignments are using auditIfNotExists and have non-compliant resources?
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policyDefinitionAction == 'auditifnotexists'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| summarize NonCompliantCount = count() by
    assignment = tostring(properties.policyAssignmentName),
    definition = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionName)
| order by NonCompliantCount desc

// Resources that became non-compliant in the last 7 days
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policyAssignmentName == 'audit-kv-diag'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| where todatetime(properties.timestamp) > ago(7d)
| project kvId = tostring(properties.resourceId), ts = todatetime(properties.timestamp)
| order by ts desc
AINE in Initiatives and the Effect Parameter Pattern

AINE policies inside an initiative can be parameterized with a shared effect parameter so the entire initiative can be toggled between AuditIfNotExists and Disabled without creating separate definitions. This is the standard pattern in Azure built-in initiatives like the CIS Microsoft Azure Foundations benchmark.

A common governance progression starts with AINE to understand the compliance baseline across an environment. Once the failure patterns are understood and the deployment template is tested, the AINE definition is replaced with an equivalent DINE definition. The AINE assignment is removed and the DINE assignment is created with a managed identity. This two-phase approach avoids deploying companion resources in environments where they may cause disruption before governance is ready.

# Phase 1: AINE — understand the baseline (run for 30–90 days)
# Assignment: audit-kv-diag (AuditIfNotExists)
# Monitor compliance state via Resource Graph / Azure Monitor dashboard

# Check how many are non-compliant
az policy state summarize \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'audit-kv-diag'"

# Phase 2: Switch to DINE — auto-remediate
# Step 1: Remove AINE assignment
az policy assignment delete \
  --name audit-kv-diag \
  --management-group mg-corp

# Step 2: Create DINE assignment (replaces AINE, now deploys diagnostic settings)
az policy assignment create \
  --name deploy-kv-diag \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --policy $DINE_DEF_ID \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus2 \
  --params '{"logAnalyticsWorkspaceId":{"value":"$LAW_ID"}}'

# Step 3: Grant the managed identity Contributor role
IDENTITY_OID=$(az policy assignment show \
  --name deploy-kv-diag \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --query 'identity.principalId' -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --assignee $IDENTITY_OID \
  --role Contributor \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp

# Step 4: Create a remediation task to fix all existing non-compliant KVs
az policy remediation create \
  --name remediate-kv-diag \
  --policy-assignment /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/deploy-kv-diag \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --resource-discovery-mode ReEvaluateCompliance
Resources are Compliant even when companion is misconfigured (wrong workspace, disabled state)
Always specify existenceCondition that validates the companion is correctly configured
AINE misses companion resources in different resource groups or at subscription scope
Set existenceScope: Subscription when companion is a singleton or in a different RG
Checking a child resource when type requires parent path
Policy never finds companion — zero matches, resources appear Compliant by default
Use the full child resource type path: Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/extensions
Using AINE when DINE is needed
Compliance never improves — engineers must manually deploy companions after each audit
Switch to DINE to auto-deploy; use AINE only when manual remediation is intentional
Cannot disable the policy for exempt subscriptions without creating a separate definition
Always add an effect parameter with allowedValues: [AuditIfNotExists, Disabled]
Extension in Failed state counts as compliant because it exists
Add field: provisioningState, equals: Succeeded to the existenceCondition

AuditIfNotExists evaluates whether a companion resource exists alongside the resource that triggered policy evaluation. It marks the primary resource NonCompliant when the companion is absent or fails the existenceCondition — but it never blocks the operation and never deploys anything, so no managed identity is required. Use AINE to establish a compliance baseline before committing to DINE auto-remediation, and always include a precise existenceCondition to avoid false compliance signals.
